narrative:
A front moved through from the northwest Sunday night. Cooler air lags behind it, but it arrives today and tonight along with a few showers this morning, that have since moved on for the most part. A stray shower or two could linger into the afternoon. Temperatures today rise only modestly—a sharp contrast to our balmy Mother’s Day weather. Tuesday has seasonable temperatures and dry weather. Then another front moves through from the northwest Wednesday with another meager round of showers (and perhaps a thunderstorm). Even cooler weather follows Wednesday night into Thursday.

This week's Monday Message covers a range of topics, from record high temperatures to pin-pointing where the best rainfall will be. Thanks to our subscribers!

The votes are in, and the winning photos have been selected. The Grand Prize Winner is a fall photo by Jason Frye of Price Lake looking toward Grandfather Mountain. See this and all the other winners at our Photo Contest Page.

Few Lingering Showers; Cooler

Last Updated Mon May 11 at 4:04PM by Jack Kendrick

A front moved through from the northwest Sunday night. Cooler air lags behind it, but it arrives today and tonight along with a few showers this morning, that have since moved on for the most part. A stray shower or two could linger into the afternoon. Temperatures today rise only modestly—a sharp contrast to our balmy Mother’s Day weather. Tuesday has seasonable temperatures and dry weather. Then another front moves through from the northwest Wednesday with another meager round of showers (and perhaps a thunderstorm). Even cooler weather follows Wednesday night into Thursday.

This week's Monday Message covers a range of topics, from record high temperatures to pin-pointing where the best rainfall will be. Thanks to our subscribers!

Forecast Discussion

A cold front moved through from the northwest Sunday night. Cooler air lags behind the front, but it arrives today along with a few showers. Showers were mostly this morning and only a few light showers remain for the afternoon. Most stay dry. Temperatures do not move much from sunrise numbers—that’s a sharp contrast to Sunday. Tonight is seasonally cool with mostly clear skies.

Tuesday, high pressure shifts from the Eastern Great Lakes to the Mid-Atlantic. By late in the day, we return to a milder southerly flow with seasonable afternoon temperatures.

Another front with a little more punch arrives Wednesday, but it does not bring much rain. Expect a few showers, mostly light, and perhaps a thunderstorm. A stray shower or two could linger into Wednesday night along the west side of the Appalachians. The air behind Wednesday’s front is colder, making Thursday a chilly day. Even frost is possible in deeper valleys Thursday night.

Our radically up-and-down weather comes to an end with this front. Friday moderates through the afternoon, and the weekend will feel like summer. I cannot rule out a stray afternoon shower or thundershower Saturday during the transition to the warmer pattern. Unseasonably chilly episodes will become difficult—or impossible—to find going forward.

May 11, 2026
WWR? The Parkway Foundation

There’s nothing quite like the Blue Ridge Parkway, and the Parkway Foundation plays a huge role in keeping it beautiful for everyone to enjoy. Join us as we sit down with the Foundation’s CEO, Carolyn, to talk about the programs and projects making a difference along the Parkway — and how you can help preserve the place we all love. #ExploreWNC

May 08, 2026
On This Date: The May 1992 Snowstorm

It is a story that long-time WNC residents have heard more than a few times in their lives. The legendary spring blizzard of May 6-8, 1992 that supposedly dropped nearly 60 inches of snow on Mount Pisgah, stranding 130+ guests and staff at the Pisgah Inn without electricity or hot water. The 60-inch total is almost certainly the measurement of a snow drift (winds gusted above 50 mph, as recounted in the image below), but totals in the 30 to 40 inch range are feasible, as Mount Mitchell's 30-inch report is the highest verifiable one from this event. Not to mention, our own Scott Stephens ventured up top on the 7th, and recalls 30 to 40 inches on the ground that day, as well as plenty of thunder and lightning on the night of the 6th.

These numbers are ludicrous for May! Sure, Mount Mitchell has recorded over 2 feet of snow more than a few times over the years, but for a total this high to occur closer to Memorial Day than March 1st is truly remarkable. To the west, Mount LeConte recorded 17 inches of snow, for its largest May snowfall on record. As the clipping above states, Asheville's 0.2 inches of snow was the first ever instance of May snowfall. Beech Mountain and Grandfather Mountain recieved more than the "five to six inches" noted in the above report, with official records closer to 8 to 12 inches. In Boone, just enough snow to make the roads slick for a while fell, but not enough to shut down the town. Even the South Carolina mountains got in on the action, with Caesar's Head recording 1.5 inches of snow!

The abnormal system was the result of an unusually strong and cold upper-level low pressure system, the same sort of system that dropped 9+ inches on high elevations back around Veteran's Day last November. The difference with the May 1992 system is that the timing is even further outside of the usual timing for snow in WNC, meaning the system must be more anomalous than our November snowmaker, and must move slowly to produce such high precipitation amounts. 30+ inch totals at Mitchell and Pisgah were recorded despite reports that precipitation flipped back and forth from rain to snow occasionally, showing the fickle nature of the springtime snowfall.

This event's late timing brought many downed trees and power lines to elevations that recieved notable snowfall, some due to wind, but some due to the weight of snow on leafed-out trees! Up at the Pisgah Inn above 5,000 feet, most trees would still be bare or just beginning to leaf out in early May, but plenty of leaves exist below 4000 feet or so, allowing for the unusual situation where snow has more surface area to collect on trees and weigh them down. This can be seen in the second photograph below, as branches sag.

The Los Angeles Times has an archived article regarding the legendary snowfall atop Mt. Pisgah, which you can read for yourself here. The story reports trapped school buses from trees and lines on the roads, as well as Floridian tourists escorted down the mountain by Haywood County police!

How Unusual Is Spring Snow?

The degree of snowfall observed at high elevations in May 1992 is about as unusual as it gets for spring snow. Heavy April snows are not unheard of, although they are not common. Heavy snows beyond mid-March are much less surprising. Let's use Mount Mitchell as our guinea pig. Put this way: the average final date of snowfall on Mt. Mitchell since 1980 is April 10th. The earliest final snowfall is February 13th, and the latest is May 14th. Snowfall of greater than a foot has been observed 6 times in April and May, with 1992 the only instance of such a total later than April 12th.

More simply put: it isn't especially unusual for early May to send a few flakes the way of the 6000 foot peaks, but it is a once-in-a-lifetime, unicorn event for the season's heaviest snowfall to occur in early May. 27.0" is tied for the 24th-heaviest 3-day snowfall on Mitchell... but only one event that occurred after the spring solstice exceeded that, with 35.0" between April 3rd and 5th, 1987.

This event was so out of the ordinary for the time of year, that the 3-day total more than doubles the total from the next-highest snowfall after Tax Day on Mt. Mitchell (11.0", 4/24-26/2005). Those are the only two events to reach double digits after April 15th.

Further to the west, Mount LeConte holds the title for the latest snowfall of any sort recorded in the Southern Appalachians. If you have ever made the trek to the summit, you may have noticed the decades of weather records available on the outside of the LeConte Lodge. Those records note that the latest snow on record occurred June 1st, 1983, in the form of flurries. Blogger Tom Layton dug through old newspaper clippings and discovered this piece of the Knoxville News Sentinel reporting snow on LeConte on June 2, 1956.

Whether that is true or not is likely impossible to verify for certain, but either way it shows that June snow is doable at the highest of heights in North Carolina and Tennessee. Enjoy this more recent image of May snowfall, atop one of the supply-carrying llamas on LeConte in 2014.

The rarity of the May 1992 snowstorm cannot be overstated, and who knows when we may see an event anywhere near that level of absurdity. Forget most impressive snowstorms, this is one of the wildest weather events of any kind in the history of the state of North Carolina.
